In C++, the code would look like:

Initialize left = 0, right = s.size()-1.

So code:

int minimumLength(string s) {
    int left = 0, right = s.length() - 1;
    while (left < right && s[left] == s[right]) {
        char c = s[left];
        while (left <= right && s[left] == c) {
            left++;
        }
        while (left <= right && s[right] == c) {
            right--;
        }
    }
    return right - left + 1;
}

int minimumLength(string s) {

}

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int minimumLength(string s) {
        int left = 0, right = s.length() - 1;
        while (left < right && s[left] == s[right]) {
            char c = s[left];
            while (left <= right && s[left] == c) left++;
            while (left <= right && s[right] == c) right--;
        }
        return max(0, right - left + 1);
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: Uses a two-pointer approach to greedily remove matching prefix and suffix characters. The left and right pointers move inward while characters match, skipping all consecutive same characters. This efficiently reduces the string in linear time with constant space. The main weakness is that it may not handle non-greedy optimal scenarios, but the problem constraints ensure greedy removal is optimal.
